figures.gloss: Slavic thunder-god — his name from PIE *Perkʷūnos — eternally striking the serpent Veles from his oak.
figures.facet: Among the best-preserved Indo-European storm names; the Baltic Perkūnas is his near-twin.
figures.provenance: Primary Chronicle (Rus-Byzantine treaty oaths of 907 and 971; Vladimir's 980 Kyiv pantheon); The Lay of Igor's Campaign.

figures.gloss: God of cattle and wealth, the watery low places, magic, poetry, and the binding of oaths — invoked jointly with Perun in the Rus-Byzantine treaty oaths of 907 and 971 ('by Perun and by Volos, the cattle-god'), though absent from Vladimir's twelve-figure 980 Kyiv pantheon.
figures.facet: Perun's own entry in this roster calls him the serpent struck from the oak — a reconstruction (Ivanov & Toporov, 1974) reading the two gods as a chaoskampf pair; the chronicle attestation itself is thinner and different in kind, an oath-god paired by name only, no combat myth recorded.
figures.provenance: Rus-Byzantine treaty oaths (Primary Chronicle, 907 and 971); V.V. Ivanov & V.N. Toporov's reconstructed Perun-Veles myth (1974); The Lay of Igor's Campaign (Boyan called 'Veles's grandson').

figures.gloss: Goddess of moist earth, spinning, weaving, and women's domestic labor — the sole goddess named among the seven gods enshrined by Prince Vladimir in Kyiv in 980, per the Primary Chronicle.
figures.facet: Her cult did not vanish with conversion but migrated: East Slavic folk practice reassigned her functions and feast-day observances (spinning taboos, offerings at wells) onto St. Paraskeva-Pyatnitsa, a folk-Christian double rather than a suppression.
figures.provenance: Primary Chronicle (Vladimir's pantheon, 980); ethnographic record of Paraskeva-Pyatnitsa folk practice, standard in Slavic folklore scholarship (e.g. Linda Ivanits).

figures.gloss: Heavenly smith-god, known almost entirely from a single interpolated passage in the Hypatian Codex's Slavic translation of John Malalas's Chronicle, which equates him with the Greek Hephaistos and names the sun-god Dazhbog as his son.
figures.facet: A deity reconstructed from a single translator's gloss rather than cult attestation — his entire recoverable profile is inherited from the equation to Hephaistos, making him the pantheon's most text-dependent figure.
figures.provenance: Hypatian Codex (Ipatiev Chronicle), interpolated Slavic translation of John Malalas's Chronicle.

figures.gloss: Sun-god, named as Svarog's son in the same Malalas-derived Hypatian Codex passage that equates him with the Greek Helios; separately attested as a member of Vladimir's 980 Kyiv pantheon and invoked in The Lay of Igor's Campaign, where the Rus' people are called 'Dazhbog's grandsons'.
figures.facet: The rare Slavic deity attested from three independent angles — chronicle pantheon, chronicler's gloss, and epic literature — which is why his solar identity is among the least disputed in the reconstruction.
figures.provenance: Primary Chronicle (980 pantheon); Hypatian Codex/Malalas gloss; The Lay of Igor's Campaign.

figures.gloss: God associated with the winds, named among Vladimir's 980 Kyiv pantheon and invoked in The Lay of Igor's Campaign, where the winds are called 'Stribog's grandsons'.
figures.facet: His entire recoverable profile rests on those two attestations; any further function assigned to him beyond 'wind-god' is later scholarly speculation, not textual record.
figures.provenance: Primary Chronicle (980 pantheon); The Lay of Igor's Campaign.

figures.gloss: Solar deity of Vladimir's 980 Kyiv pantheon, listed immediately alongside Dazhbog; his name is usually derived from an Iranian word for 'sun' (compare Avestan/Old Persian xᵛar-), suggesting steppe-nomadic (Scythian or Alanic) contact or borrowing.
figures.facet: His pairing with Dazhbog produces the pantheon's own unresolved puzzle — two solar gods enshrined together — read by scholars as either a foreign sun-name layered onto a native one or two distinct solar functions never reconciled in the sources.
figures.provenance: Primary Chronicle (980 pantheon); comparative Iranian etymology as discussed in standard Slavic mythology scholarship (e.g. Mallory & Adams, Encyclopedia of Indo-European Culture).

figures.gloss: Winged canine-bird figure among Vladimir's 980 Kyiv pantheon, commonly linked by name to the Iranian Simurgh; most reconstructions read him as a guardian of seeds, crops, or growth, though the chronicle itself assigns him no described function.
figures.facet: The pantheon's thinnest member: a name, a probable foreign loan, and a guessed-at function — the honest gloss stops at 'attested but undescribed'.
figures.provenance: Primary Chronicle (980 pantheon); comparative Iranian etymology (Simurgh) discussed in standard scholarship (Mallory & Adams).

figures.gloss: Four-headed sovereign god of the Rani at the Arkona temple on Rügen, described by the Danish chronicler Saxo Grammaticus (Gesta Danorum, c. 1200): his mead-horn was read each year to divine the harvest, his white sacred horse's steps before battle divined the fortune of war, and his temple was destroyed by Danish forces under Bishop Absalon in 1168.
figures.facet: The best-documented cult of any West Slavic deity — a named temple, a named destruction date, and an eyewitness-adjacent chronicler — because his fall coincided with the literate Christian conquest that recorded it.
figures.provenance: Saxo Grammaticus, Gesta Danorum (Book XIV, on the fall of Arkona, 1168).

figures.gloss: Three-headed god of the West Slavic Pomeranians, worshipped at Szczecin and Wolin; described in the twelfth-century biographies of Bishop Otto of Bamberg (by Ebbo and Herbord) as having gilded heads with covered eyes and mouth, and a black horse whose stepping over crossed spears was read for omens before military campaigns.
figures.facet: His three heads are consistently glossed by the missionary-biographers as rule over 'heaven, earth, and the underworld' — though that reading comes from Christian authors explaining a pagan cult to a Latin audience, not from any surviving native gloss.
figures.provenance: Ebbo and Herbord, Vitae of Otto of Bamberg (12th century).

figures.gloss: Ancestral fate-figures condemned by name in East Slavic church sermons against paganism (Slovo ob idolakh and related texts): 'Rod' ('kin/lineage') alongside the Rozhanitsy ('birth-givers'), honored with meals of bread, cheese, and honey, especially at childbirth.
figures.facet: Attested only negatively, through clerical condemnation rather than any pagan text of their own — a modern maximalist reading (Boris Rybakov, 1981) elevates Rod to supreme all-father status, a claim the medieval sermons themselves do not support.
figures.provenance: Medieval East Slavic sermons against paganism (Slovo ob idolakh and related 'Word Against...' texts); Boris Rybakov, Yazychestvo drevnikh slavyan (1981), noted here as a later maximalist reading rather than primary evidence.

figures.gloss: Straw effigy of winter and death, dressed and carried in procession out of the village by young people, then drowned in a river or pond (or burned) as spring approaches — still performed today in Poland and neighboring Slavic lands, typically around the spring equinox or the fourth Sunday of Lent.
figures.facet: A folk practice with unbroken continuity into the present, unlike the chronicle deities reconstructed from medieval condemnation — here the rite itself, not scholarly inference, is the evidence.
figures.provenance: Ethnographic record of Polish and Czech spring folk custom (topienie Marzanny); standard treatments in Slavic folklore scholarship.

figures.gloss: East Slavic midsummer festival of bonfire-leaping, ritual river bathing, wreath-floating, and the legendary quest for the once-blooming fern flower; continuously attested from medieval sermons condemning it through to modern folk observance, later reassigned to the feast of St. John the Baptist (Ivan Kupala).
figures.facet: One of the clearest cases of a pagan seasonal rite surviving Christianization by relocation onto a saint's calendar day rather than suppression.
figures.provenance: Medieval sermons against paganism referencing Kupala revelry; ethnographic record of Ivan Kupala observance across East Slavic lands.

edges.note: Both inherit the reconstructed PIE storm-god's serpent-slaying myth (*Perkʷūnos vs. the serpent).
Shared Indo-European thunderer pattern: hammer/axe, oak, and the serpent at the world's root.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Thunder deities who strike the earth with lightning, heavily associated with martial prowess and high mountains.
Perkūnas and Perun both reflect *Perkwunos within the Balto-Slavic node — the closest cognate pair in the whole storm-cluster, oak and thunder shared.
GOLD SEAM: Fierce, storm-associated war deities intimately linked to the bull and invoked to secure military victory.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The active, thunderous sky-deity who directly manages the rains, fertility, and the seasonal renewal of the earth.
Sky-father storm-wielders of unrelated families converging on the same joint, both defined by the thunderbolt at the head of their pantheons.

Both supreme storm-fathers of the northern forests wielding axe and thunderbolt; Slavic contact may have coloured the Finnic image, but Perun's *Perkwunos name has no Uralic descendant — a borrowed motif at most, not shared blood.
Both are dragon-slaying storm-sovereigns of the Indo-European thunder-type; the seam is structural here, the firmer cognate running through the *Perkwunos reconstruction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are fierce, atmospheric warrior deities who commanded the absolute martial devotion of their respective tribes.
GOLD SEAM: the thunder-god as guarantor of oaths and smiter of the false — Slavic Perun and Igbo Amadioha, the storm wielded as the impartial arm of cosmic law across wholly unrelated families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are violent, mountaintop storm-wielders whose cultural imprint was so strong they named the local word for Thursday.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A warrior deity who oversees oaths, protects treaties, and mediates between humanity and the cosmic order.

Two thunder-and-rain high gods who give the storm and the water on which life depends; both ride the cloud and answer the prayer for rain. Functional kin of the storm-wielder office across unrelated families, never descent.

Both name the storm and the thunder as a high honoured power; Pillán's facet is that the bolt and the eruption are the powerful dead themselves, not Perun's distinct sky-warrior — storm-wielding ancestor against storm-wielding god.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are dynamic, axe-wielding thunderers who serve as the beloved, active protectors of their respective peoples.

Hammer-and-bolt thunder-gods of the northern forests who smite the powers of evil; a structural rhyme with the Slavic storm-father, but Perun's *Perkwunos name leaves no Uralic descendant — the pattern shared, not the blood.
GOLD SEAM: Fierce, militant storm-wielding deities of the Balkans and Eastern Europe who engage in direct physical combat.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The tempest deity who commands lightning and serves as the active, martial force of the skies.
The defining revival-of seam: modern Rodnovery deliberately restores the chronicle-attested Perun to his 980 station — a documented reach back across a thousand-year break, not a continuity.

both are storm-associated combatants locked in a recurring dragon/serpent-combat myth tied to drought or flood — Slavic Perun against Veles, Albanian Drangue against Kulshedra — independent chaoskampf cycles across unrelated language families.
Slavic Perun continues *Perkʷūnos directly, the closest surviving cognate pair in the whole IE storm-cluster along with Perkūnas.

The chronicle's own joint oath ('by Perun and by Volos') and a reconstructed storm-serpent duel together form the pantheon's central polarity — the only intra-tradition edge this roster can now draw for its own core myth.

Veles and Baltic Velnias share the reconstructed *wel- root (Ivanov & Toporov) tying them to a common Balto-Slavic chthonic figure — the same equation Rodnovery's revived Veles already carries to this Baltic node.
The Hypatian Codex's Malalas-derived interpolation explicitly equates Svarog with Hephaistos — a medieval chronicler's own act of cross-cultural equation, not a modern comparison.
The same Hypatian Codex passage names Dazhbog, Svarog's son, as the sun and equates him with Helios — the chronicler's own equation, inherited whole.

Neighboring Balto-Slavic earth-goddesses of moist ground and women's domestic labor, structurally paired the way Rod's Rozhanitsy already pair with Baltic Laima at birth — a folklore parallel, not proven descent.

The defining revival-of seam: modern Rodnovery restores the sermon-condemned Rod and Rozhanitsy to supreme-god status, following Rybakov's 1981 maximalist reading — a documented reach back, not a continuity, matching the same pattern as Perun's own revival bridge.

Both are West Slavic multi-headed sovereign gods known through horse-divination cult practice recorded by contemporary chroniclers (Saxo Grammaticus for Arkona, Ebbo/Herbord for Szczecin and Wolin) — the two best-documented pillars of the same regional cult-type.

Two independent pre-Christian thunder gods whose storm-and-harvest functions were absorbed into the cult of St. Elijah after Christianization — Ossetian Uacilla (Uac-Illa) and Slavic Perun, whose power migrated to Ilya Prorok in East Slavic folk religion; parallel process, no direct contact.

dossier.essence: The pre-Christian religion of the Slavic peoples of eastern and southeastern Europe, from the first millennium CE until Christianization — the baptism of Kievan Rus in 988 and the gradual conversion of the rest. No native account survives, so it must be reconstructed from Byzantine, Arab, and Latin chronicles, later East-Slavic sermons against paganism, place-names, folklore, and comparative Indo-European evidence.
dossier.cosmos: The evidence is fragmentary and no unified system is securely attested. At its head stands a thunder-god, Perun — heir to the Indo-European storm-deity *Perkʷūnos — whose reconstructed adversary is the serpentine, cattle-and-underworld god Veles; the Rus' Primary Chronicle records Prince Vladimir erecting a pantheon at Kiev in 980 (Perun, Khors, Dazhbog, Stribog, Simargl, and the goddess Mokosh). Scholars posit an opposition of sky and underworld and a strong ancestor cult, but a coherent doctrine of the afterlife is not reliably preserved.
dossier.practice: Worship centered on open-air sanctuaries, carved idols, and sacred groves, springs, and hilltops, with sacrifice and seasonal feasts tied to the farming year. The best-described institution is the western, Baltic-Slavic temple cult — the sanctuary of Sventovit at Arkona on Rügen, with its great idol and oracle, destroyed by the Danes in 1168. Ancestor veneration and household spirits persisted for centuries beneath official Christianity as folk custom.
dossier.canon: None native; the sources are external and later — the Rus' Primary Chronicle, Procopius, Ibn Fadlan, Thietmar of Merseburg, and Saxo Grammaticus and Helmold on the Baltic Slavs — supplemented by anti-pagan sermons and by ethnographic folklore.
dossier.reflects: A faith known almost only from the outside — the storm-god at the summit and the long afterlife of ancestral and seasonal custom beneath an imposed Christianity.
sources: Judith Kalik & Alexander Uchitel, 'Slavic Gods and Heroes' (2019)
